intro_text: Hi there! 😊 Today we explore Coordinate Geometry — the beautiful bridge between algebra and geometry! Every point in a plane has an address — and we'll learn to read and write those addresses perfectly! 🎯
complete_message: You have mastered Coordinate Geometry! You can now locate any point in the universe using just two numbers 💪

explain_questions.question: What is the Cartesian Plane and how was it invented?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the Cartesian Plane and how was it invented?

steps.t: The Cartesian Plane was invented by the French mathematician René Descartes. He noticed a fly on his ceiling and realised he could describe its exact position using two numbers — its distances from two walls!
steps.s: René Descartes invented coordinate geometry by observing a fly on his ceiling and describing its position using two distances.

steps.t: Two perpendicular lines are drawn: the horizontal line is called the X-axis, and the vertical line is called the Y-axis. They meet at a point called the Origin, denoted by O. The coordinates of the origin are (0, 0).
steps.s: The horizontal line is the x-axis. The vertical line is the y-axis. They meet at the origin with coordinates 0, 0.

steps.t: The plane is divided into 4 regions called Quadrants, numbered I, II, III, IV going anticlockwise. Quadrant I: x>0, y>0. Quadrant II: x<0, y>0. Quadrant III: x<0, y<0. Quadrant IV: x>0, y<0.
steps.s: The four quadrants go anticlockwise. Quadrant 1 is top-right with both positive. Quadrant 2 is top-left with x negative.

explain_questions.confirm_question: What are the coordinates of the origin?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: What are the coordinates of the origin?
nudges: The origin is where the x-axis and y-axis meet
At that point, the distance from both axes is zero
Answer: (0, 0)
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="cg1s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Descartes: fly on ceiling → describe position with 2 numbers</text></g><g id="cg1s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">X-axis (horizontal) | Y-axis (vertical) | Origin O(0,0)</text></g><g id="cg1s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Q1:+,+ | Q2:−,+ | Q3:−,− | Q4:+,− (anticlockwise)</text></g><g id="cg1ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Origin = (0, 0)</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What are coordinates of a point and what is abscissa and ordinate?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are coordinates of a point and what is abscissa and ordinate?

steps.t: Every point in the Cartesian plane is represented by an ordered pair (x, y). The first number x is the distance from the Y-axis (horizontal distance). The second number y is the distance from the X-axis (vertical distance).
steps.s: A point is written as ordered pair x comma y. x is horizontal distance from y-axis. y is vertical distance from x-axis.

steps.t: The x-coordinate is called the ABSCISSA. The y-coordinate is called the ORDINATE. Together they are the COORDINATES of the point. For point P(3, 5): abscissa = 3, ordinate = 5.
steps.s: The x-coordinate is called abscissa. The y-coordinate is called ordinate.

steps.t: Important: Order matters! (3, 5) and (5, 3) are DIFFERENT points. (3,5) means move 3 right then 5 up. (5,3) means move 5 right then 3 up. Always write x first, then y!
steps.s: The order matters. 3 comma 5 and 5 comma 3 are different points. Always write x first.

explain_questions.confirm_question: For point P(4, −3), what is the abscissa and what is the ordinate?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: For the point P 4 comma negative 3, what is the abscissa and what is the ordinate?
nudges: Abscissa = x-coordinate = first number
Ordinate = y-coordinate = second number
Abscissa = 4, Ordinate = −3
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="cg2s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Point (x,y): x = dist from Y-axis, y = dist from X-axis</text></g><g id="cg2s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Abscissa = x-coord | Ordinate = y-coord</text></g><g id="cg2s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">(3,5) ≠ (5,3) — ORDER MATTERS!</text></g><g id="cg2ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Abscissa=4, Ordinate=−3</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you plot a point on the Cartesian plane?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you plot a point on the Cartesian plane?

steps.t: To plot point P(3, 4): Start at origin O(0,0). Move 3 units to the RIGHT along the X-axis (positive x means right). Then move 4 units UP (positive y means up). Mark the point — that is P(3, 4) in Quadrant I!
steps.s: Start at origin. Move 3 right. Then 4 up. That is P 3 comma 4 in Quadrant 1.

steps.t: To plot Q(−2, 3): Start at O. Move 2 units to the LEFT (negative x). Then 3 units UP (positive y). Q(−2,3) lies in Quadrant II.
steps.s: Negative x means move left. Positive y means move up. Q negative 2 comma 3 is in Quadrant 2.

steps.t: To plot R(−1, −4): Move 1 LEFT and 4 DOWN. Quadrant III. To plot S(2, −3): Move 2 RIGHT and 3 DOWN. Quadrant IV. Memory trick: Think of a compass — right=east, left=west, up=north, down=south!
steps.s: Negative y means move down. Use compass directions to remember. Right is east, up is north, left is west, down is south.

explain_questions.confirm_question: In which quadrant does the point (−3, 5) lie?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: In which quadrant does the point negative 3 comma 5 lie?
nudges: x = −3 (negative) means the point is to the LEFT of y-axis
y = 5 (positive) means the point is ABOVE the x-axis
Left and above = Quadrant II
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="cg3s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">P(3,4): right 3, up 4 → Quadrant I</text></g><g id="cg3s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Q(−2,3): left 2, up 3 → Quadrant II</text></g><g id="cg3s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">R(−1,−4): left,down=QIII | S(2,−3): right,down=QIV</text></g><g id="cg3ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">(−3,5): x negative, y positive → Quadrant II</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What are the signs of coordinates in each quadrant?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are the signs of coordinates in each quadrant?

steps.t: Quadrant I (top-right): x positive, y positive → (+, +). Example: (3, 4), (1, 7). This is where both distances are measured in positive directions.
steps.s: Quadrant 1 is top-right. Both x and y are positive.

steps.t: Quadrant II (top-left): x negative, y positive → (−, +). Example: (−2, 5), (−4, 1). Quadrant III (bottom-left): x negative, y negative → (−, −). Example: (−3, −2).
steps.s: Quadrant 2 top-left: x negative y positive. Quadrant 3 bottom-left: both negative.

steps.t: Quadrant IV (bottom-right): x positive, y negative → (+, −). Example: (4, −3). Memory trick: 'All Schools Teach Children' — All positive in Q1, Sine positive in Q2, Tan positive in Q3, Cos positive in Q4!
steps.s: Quadrant 4 bottom-right: x positive y negative. Remember All Schools Teach Children for the signs.

explain_questions.confirm_question: A point has x > 0 and y < 0. In which quadrant does it lie?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: A point has x greater than 0 and y less than 0. In which quadrant does it lie?
nudges: x > 0 means positive x → right side of y-axis
y < 0 means negative y → below x-axis
Right and below = Quadrant IV
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="cg4s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="185" height="50" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb"/><text x="102" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Q II: (−,+)</text><text x="102" y="46" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#7a8c6e">top-left</text></g><g id="cg4s0b" opacity="0"><rect x="215" y="8" width="185" height="50" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="312" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Q I: (+,+)</text><text x="312" y="46" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#b85c2a">top-right</text></g><g id="cg4s1" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="66" width="185" height="50" rx="7" fill="#fff5f0"/><text x="102" y="86" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#b85c2a">Q III: (−,−)</text><text x="102" y="104" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#b85c2a">bottom-left</text></g><g id="cg4s2" opacity="0"><rect x="215" y="66" width="185" height="50" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb"/><text x="312" y="86" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Q IV: (+,−)</text><text x="312" y="104" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#7a8c6e">bottom-right</text></g><g id="cg4ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">x>0, y<0 → Quadrant IV (+,−)</text></g>

explain_questions.question: Where do points lie when they are on the axes?
explain_questions.question_spoken: Where do points lie when they are on the axes?

steps.t: Points ON the X-axis have y-coordinate = 0. Example: (3, 0), (−2, 0), (5, 0) all lie on the X-axis. They are NOT in any quadrant — they are ON the axis.
steps.s: Points on the x-axis have y equals zero. They are not in any quadrant.

steps.t: Points ON the Y-axis have x-coordinate = 0. Example: (0, 4), (0, −3), (0, 7) all lie on the Y-axis. The origin O(0,0) lies on BOTH axes simultaneously.
steps.s: Points on the y-axis have x equals zero. The origin lies on both axes.

steps.t: Key rule: if x=0 and y≠0 → Y-axis. If y=0 and x≠0 → X-axis. If x=0 and y=0 → Origin. If neither is zero → in one of the 4 quadrants. This is a very common exam question!
steps.s: If x is zero, the point is on the y-axis. If y is zero, the point is on the x-axis. If both are zero, it is the origin.

explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Where does the point 0 comma negative 5 lie?
nudges: The x-coordinate is 0
When x=0, the point lies on the Y-axis
Answer: On the Y-axis (negative side, since y=−5)
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="cg5s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">On X-axis → y=0: (3,0),(−2,0),(5,0)</text></g><g id="cg5s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">On Y-axis → x=0: (0,4),(0,−3),(0,7)</text></g><g id="cg5s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">x=0→Y-axis | y=0→X-axis | x=y=0→Origin</text></g><g id="cg5ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">(0,−5): x=0 → lies on Y-axis</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you read the coordinates of a plotted point?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you read the coordinates of a plotted point?

steps.t: To read coordinates of a plotted point: first draw a perpendicular from the point to the X-axis. Where it meets the X-axis gives the x-coordinate (abscissa).
steps.s: Drop a perpendicular to the x-axis. Where it meets gives the x-coordinate.

steps.t: Then draw a perpendicular from the point to the Y-axis. Where it meets the Y-axis gives the y-coordinate (ordinate). Together they form the ordered pair (x, y).
steps.s: Drop a perpendicular to the y-axis. Where it meets gives the y-coordinate.

steps.t: Example: A point lies 4 units right of Y-axis and 3 units above X-axis → coordinates (4, 3). A point 2 units left and 5 units below → coordinates (−2, −5). Always check the sign!
steps.s: Right is positive x, left is negative x, up is positive y, down is negative y. Always check the sign.

explain_questions.confirm_question: A point is 5 units to the left of Y-axis and 2 units above X-axis. What are its coordinates?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: A point is 5 units to the left of the y-axis and 2 units above the x-axis. What are its coordinates?
nudges: Left of Y-axis → x is negative → x = −5
Above X-axis → y is positive → y = 2
Answer: (−5, 2)
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="cg6s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Drop perpendicular to X-axis → get x-coordinate</text></g><g id="cg6s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Drop perpendicular to Y-axis → get y-coordinate</text></g><g id="cg6s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">4 right, 3 up → (4,3) | 2 left, 5 down → (−2,−5)</text></g><g id="cg6ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">5 left, 2 up → (−5, 2)</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What are the properties of the axes and quadrants?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are the properties of the axes and quadrants?

steps.t: The X-axis and Y-axis are perpendicular to each other (at 90°). They divide the plane into 4 quadrants. The positive X-axis points RIGHT and the negative X-axis points LEFT.
steps.s: The x-axis and y-axis are perpendicular. Positive x-axis is to the right. Negative x-axis is to the left.

steps.t: The positive Y-axis points UP and the negative Y-axis points DOWN. Any point in the plane can be uniquely identified by its coordinates (x, y). No two different points have the same coordinates!
steps.s: Positive y-axis is upward. Negative y-axis is downward. Every point has a unique address.

steps.t: The axes themselves are NOT part of any quadrant. Points on the axes belong to the axes, not to quadrants. This distinction is important in CBSE exams — axes and quadrants are separate!
steps.s: The axes are not part of any quadrant. Points on axes belong to the axes, not to quadrants.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Do the axes belong to any quadrant? Yes or No.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Do the axes belong to any quadrant? Yes or No.
nudges: The axes divide the plane into quadrants
But the axes themselves are separate from the quadrants
Answer: No, axes do not belong to any quadrant
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="cg7s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">X and Y axes are perpendicular (90°)</text></g><g id="cg7s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Every point has UNIQUE coordinates</text></g><g id="cg7s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Axes are NOT part of any quadrant!</text></g><g id="cg7ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">No — axes belong to neither quadrant</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How does a street plan use coordinate geometry?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How does a street plan use coordinate geometry?

steps.t: Real-life use: A city has two main roads crossing at centre — one North-South and one East-West. These are like Y-axis and X-axis. Other streets run parallel, 200m apart.
steps.s: Two perpendicular main roads form natural axes. Parallel streets create a grid.

steps.t: Like a Cartesian plane, every intersection has an address! Cross street (3, 4) means 3rd street from Y-axis direction and 4th from X-axis direction. This is exactly coordinate geometry!
steps.s: Every street intersection has a coordinate address just like points on the Cartesian plane.

steps.t: This is why Descartes invented coordinate geometry! Real maps, GPS, computer screens — all use the same principle of two perpendicular reference lines. Coordinate geometry is all around us!
steps.s: GPS systems, digital maps and computer screens all use coordinate geometry with two perpendicular references.

explain_questions.confirm_question: In a street plan, the two main roads form what in coordinate geometry?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: In a street plan, the two main roads form what in coordinate geometry?
nudges: The two perpendicular main roads are like the X and Y axes
They create a grid exactly like the Cartesian plane
Answer: The coordinate axes (x-axis and y-axis)
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="cg8s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">N-S road = Y-axis | E-W road = X-axis</text></g><g id="cg8s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Cross street (3,4) = 3 right, 4 up from centre</text></g><g id="cg8s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">GPS, maps, screens — all use coordinate geometry!</text></g><g id="cg8ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">The two roads form the coordinate axes</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you describe the position of a point in words?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you describe the position of a point in words?

steps.t: To describe P(3, 4): 'P is 3 units to the right of Y-axis and 4 units above X-axis' OR 'abscissa is 3, ordinate is 4'. P lies in Quadrant I.
steps.s: Describe each coordinate as a distance from an axis. State the quadrant.

steps.t: To describe Q(−2, 5): 'Q is 2 units to the LEFT of Y-axis and 5 units above X-axis. Abscissa = −2, ordinate = 5. Q lies in Quadrant II.'
steps.s: Negative x means to the left. Positive y means above. This point is in Quadrant 2.

steps.t: To describe R(4, 0): 'R lies on the X-axis, 4 units to the right of origin.' When ordinate = 0, point is on X-axis. When abscissa = 0, point is on Y-axis. State this clearly!
steps.s: When ordinate is zero, state the point lies on the x-axis. When abscissa is zero, state it lies on the y-axis.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Describe the position of point (0, 7) in words.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Describe the position of the point 0 comma 7 in words.
nudges: x-coordinate = 0 means the point is on the Y-axis
y-coordinate = 7 means 7 units above the origin
Answer: On the Y-axis, 7 units above the origin
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="cg9s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">P(3,4): 3 right of Y-axis, 4 above X-axis → Q1</text></g><g id="cg9s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Q(−2,5): 2 left, 5 above → Q2</text></g><g id="cg9s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">R(4,0): ON X-axis, 4 right of origin</text></g><g id="cg9ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">(0,7): ON Y-axis, 7 units above origin</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do signs of coordinates tell you the quadrant?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do signs of coordinates tell you the quadrant?

steps.t: The signs of (x, y) tell you the quadrant instantly: (+,+)=Q1, (−,+)=Q2, (−,−)=Q3, (+,−)=Q4. This is one of the fastest tricks in coordinate geometry!
steps.s: Plus plus is Quadrant 1. Minus plus is Quadrant 2. Minus minus is Quadrant 3. Plus minus is Quadrant 4.

steps.t: Example: If a point has x = −4 and y = −7, it's in Quadrant III (both negative). If x = 5 and y = −2, it's in Quadrant IV (x positive, y negative). No plotting needed!
steps.s: Just check the signs. Both negative means Quadrant 3. Positive x and negative y means Quadrant 4.

steps.t: Special: if xy > 0 (same sign), point is in Q1 or Q3. If xy < 0 (opposite signs), point is in Q2 or Q4. If x or y = 0, point is on an axis. These are olympiad-level tricks!
steps.s: If x times y is positive, the point is in Quadrant 1 or 3. If negative, it is in Quadrant 2 or 4.

explain_questions.confirm_question: In which quadrant does a point with x < 0 and y < 0 lie?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: In which quadrant does a point with x less than 0 and y less than 0 lie?
nudges: x < 0 means left of y-axis (negative x)
y < 0 means below x-axis (negative y)
Both negative → Quadrant III
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="cg10s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">(+,+)=Q1 | (−,+)=Q2 | (−,−)=Q3 | (+,−)=Q4</text></g><g id="cg10s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">(−4,−7)→Q3 | (5,−2)→Q4 — no plotting needed!</text></g><g id="cg10s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">xy>0 → Q1 or Q3 | xy<0 → Q2 or Q4</text></g><g id="cg10ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">x<0, y<0 → Quadrant III</text></g>

practice_questions.question: What are the coordinates of the origin?
practice_questions.question_spoken: What are the coordinates of the origin?
steps: The origin is where the x-axis and y-axis intersect
At this point, the distance from both axes is zero
practice_questions.trick: Origin = O = (0,0). It's the starting point of the entire coordinate system. Both coordinates are zero!
practice_questions.alt: The word 'origin' means beginning. It is where everything starts — at distance zero from both axes. So (0,0).

practice_questions.question: In which quadrant does the point (−4, 3) lie?
practice_questions.question_spoken: In which quadrant does the point negative 4 comma 3 lie?
practice_questions.trick: Check signs: (−,+) is always Quadrant II. Left+Up=QII. Remember: All Schools Teach Children!
practice_questions.alt: Draw the axes. Go 4 left, then 3 up. You land in the top-left region — that is Quadrant II.

practice_questions.question: What is the abscissa of the point (7, −3)?
practice_questions.question_spoken: What is the abscissa of the point 7 comma negative 3?
steps: Abscissa = x-coordinate = the first number in the ordered pair
practice_questions.trick: Abscissa = x-coordinate = FIRST number. Ordinate = y-coordinate = SECOND number. Never mix them up!
practice_questions.alt: Think: ABscissa = comes Before ordinate. A comes before O in the alphabet. x comes before y. Abscissa = x = first number.

practice_questions.question: Point (5, 0) lies on which axis?
practice_questions.question_spoken: The point 5 comma 0 lies on which axis?
steps: When y=0, the point lies on the X-axis
(5, 0) is on the X-axis, 5 units to the right of origin
practice_questions.trick: y=0 → X-axis. x=0 → Y-axis. The zero coordinate tells you which axis the point is on!
practice_questions.alt: The x-axis is the horizontal line where y=0 everywhere. Since this point has y=0, it must be on the x-axis.

practice_questions.question: What are the signs of coordinates in Quadrant III?
practice_questions.question_spoken: What are the signs of coordinates in Quadrant III?
steps: Quadrant III is the bottom-left region
So both x and y are negative: (−,−)
practice_questions.trick: Q1(+,+) Q2(−,+) Q3(−,−) Q4(+,−). Going anticlockwise, the pattern changes systematically!
practice_questions.alt: In the bottom-left of the plane, you have gone left (negative x) and down (negative y). So both coordinates are negative.

practice_questions.question: Which of these points lies in Quadrant IV? A:(−3,−2)  B:(4,−5)  C:(−1,6)  D:(3,4)
practice_questions.question_spoken: Which of these points lies in Quadrant IV? A: negative 3 comma negative 2. B: 4 comma negative 5. C: negative 1 comma 6. D: 3 comma 4.
steps: B: (4,−5) x positive, y negative → Q4 ✓
C: (−1,6) x negative, y positive → Q2
practice_questions.trick: Quadrant IV has the sign pattern (+,−). Check each option for positive x and negative y!
practice_questions.alt: Q4 is bottom-right. Only option B has positive x (right) and negative y (below). So B is in Q4.

practice_questions.question: A point is 3 units above the x-axis and on the y-axis. What are its coordinates?
practice_questions.question_spoken: A point is 3 units above the x-axis and on the y-axis. What are its coordinates?
practice_questions.trick: On y-axis → x=0 always. Then the y-coordinate is just the given distance (positive if above, negative if below).
practice_questions.alt: The y-axis is where x=0. The point is 3 above the x-axis so y=3. Therefore coordinates are (0,3).

practice_questions.question: If a point has x-coordinate 0 and y-coordinate −6, where does it lie?
practice_questions.question_spoken: If a point has x-coordinate 0 and y-coordinate negative 6, where does it lie?
steps: x = 0 → point lies on the Y-axis
Point (0, −6) is on the negative Y-axis
practice_questions.trick: x=0 always means Y-axis. The sign of y tells you which side — positive y is above origin, negative y is below.
practice_questions.alt: Draw it: go 0 left/right (stay on y-axis), go 6 down. You're at (0,−6) on the negative part of the y-axis.

practice_questions.question: In which quadrant does xy > 0? Give both possible quadrants.
practice_questions.question_spoken: In which quadrant is xy greater than 0? Give both possible quadrants.
steps: xy > 0 means x and y have the SAME sign
So xy > 0 in Q1 and Q3
practice_questions.trick: xy > 0 means same sign → Q1 or Q3. xy < 0 means opposite signs → Q2 or Q4. This is an olympiad-level thinking trick!
practice_questions.alt: If xy is positive, either both are positive (Q1) or both are negative (Q3). In Q2 and Q4, x and y have opposite signs making xy negative.

practice_questions.question: What are the coordinates of a point 4 units to the right of Y-axis and 6 units below X-axis?
practice_questions.question_spoken: What are the coordinates of a point 4 units to the right of the y-axis and 6 units below the x-axis?
steps: 4 units to the right of Y-axis → x = +4
practice_questions.trick: Right = positive x. Left = negative x. Above = positive y. Below = negative y. Apply this to convert words to coordinates!
practice_questions.alt: Right of y-axis means positive x direction: x=4. Below x-axis means negative y direction: y=−6. So (4,−6).

practice_questions.question: The point (−2, 7) lies in which quadrant?
practice_questions.question_spoken: The point negative 2 comma 7 lies in which quadrant?
practice_questions.trick: (−,+) is always Q2. Signs tell quadrant instantly — no need to draw or plot!
practice_questions.alt: Negative x, positive y. Left and up. That is the top-left region of the Cartesian plane — Quadrant II.

practice_questions.question: What is the ordinate of the point (−8, 15)?
practice_questions.question_spoken: What is the ordinate of the point negative 8 comma 15?
steps: Ordinate = y-coordinate = the second number in the ordered pair
practice_questions.trick: Ordinate = y-coordinate = SECOND number. Always the second one in the brackets!
practice_questions.alt: Ordinate comes after abscissa (x). So ordinate = y = the second number. Here second number = 15.

practice_questions.question: Name the quadrant in which both coordinates are positive.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Name the quadrant in which both coordinates are positive.
steps: Both x and y positive means (+,+)
This is the top-right region of the Cartesian plane
practice_questions.trick: Q1 = (+,+) = top-right. This is the simplest quadrant rule and appears in almost every exam!
practice_questions.alt: Positive x means right, positive y means up. Right and up together = top-right = Quadrant I.

practice_questions.question: How many quadrants are there in the Cartesian plane?
practice_questions.question_spoken: How many quadrants are there in the Cartesian plane?
steps: The x-axis and y-axis divide the plane
Each axis divides the plane into 2 halves
Two perpendicular lines create 4 regions — the 4 quadrants
practice_questions.trick: Two lines crossing always create 4 angles/regions. The x and y axes are those two lines. Always 4 quadrants!
practice_questions.alt: Think of a plus sign (+). It divides space into 4 parts. The x and y axes form exactly such a plus sign.

practice_questions.question: In which quadrant does a point lie if its x-coordinate and y-coordinate have opposite signs?
practice_questions.question_spoken: In which quadrant does a point lie if its x-coordinate and y-coordinate have opposite signs?
steps: Opposite signs means one positive, one negative
So: Quadrant II or Quadrant IV
practice_questions.trick: Same signs → Q1 or Q3. Opposite signs → Q2 or Q4. This is the key pattern to remember!
practice_questions.alt: If xy < 0 (opposite signs), you're in Q2 or Q4. These are the two quadrants where x and y don't match in sign.
